summary: Fort Collins' verified affordable-housing brief now rests on direct source fetches for Housing Catalyst's public-housing-authority role, permanent-supportive and affordable-rental projects, CHFA tax-credit awards, the Land Bank program, city development incentives, Proposition 123 land-banking funds, and the 2024 repeal of U+2 occupancy limits.

sections.heading: Housing Catalyst and recent affordable-housing projects

paragraphs.text: HUD's MTW supplement identifies Housing Catalyst as Fort Collins Housing Authority and says it is the public housing authority for the City of Fort Collins. The same filing describes Housing Catalyst as a mission-driven real-estate developer and the largest property-management company in northern Colorado.

Housing Catalyst joined HUD's Moving to Work Demonstration Program through the Landlord Incentives Cohort: the agency's 2022 announcement said it was one of 29 PHAs selected, while the HUD supplement documents landlord-incentive activities tied to Housing Choice Voucher administration.

Direct-fetched Housing Catalyst project pages support a smaller retained portfolio snapshot: Redtail Ponds opened in 2015 as northern Colorado's first permanent supportive housing community with 60 apartments; Mason Place opened in January 2021 with 60 permanent supportive apartments; Oak 140 opened in January 2023 with 79 downtown apartments through a DDA partnership; Village on Impala will provide 86 affordable homes through four new apartment buildings and 12 renovated duplexes; and Village on Eastbrook is planned as a 73-unit, all-electric community with 44 one-bedroom and 29 two-bedroom apartments.

sections.heading: Housing goals and Land Use Code

paragraphs.text: Fort Collins' Land Use Code Article 5 ties affordable-housing standards to the city's adopted Housing Strategic Plan goal of reaching 10% deed-restricted affordable housing stock by 2040. KUNC's direct-fetched coverage separately reports that Fort Collins adopted a new Land Use Code in October 2023 after a long rewrite process centered in part on affordable-housing options.

CHFA's 2024 Round Two announcement lists Grove in Bloom in Fort Collins with Pedcor Investments as developer. CHFA's applicant report and September 2025 TEFRA notice support the retained project details: 264 units in the Bloom Community near East Mulberry Street and Greenfield Court and a bond amount not exceeding $64,992,920.

CHFA's 2025 Round One announcement lists Switchgrass Crossing in Fort Collins with Volunteers of America National Services as developer. Mile High CRE's direct-fetched award table supports the retained detail: 45 units, 39 one-bedroom and 6 two-bedroom apartments, income bands from 30% to 60% AMI, a $1,600,000 federal 9% credit, a $650,000 standard state credit, and a $58,500 transit-oriented communities credit.

CHFA's 2025 Round Two announcement lists Village on Eastbrook in Fort Collins with Housing Catalyst as developer and a $1,251,131 federal 4% credit. Housing Catalyst's project announcement adds the $1,800,000 accelerated state credit, the 73-unit mix, 30% to 80% AMI affordability range, all-electric design, and solar energy system.

The City describes its Land Bank as Fort Collins' only long-term affordable-housing tool: it buys sites that lack infrastructure, holds them as surrounding infrastructure is built, and points to Municipal Code Chapter 23, Article XIII as the program authority. The current city page says the city has bought seven Land Bank sites, sold two for affordable housing, and that the newest parcel was acquired in 2025.

Kechter Townhomes is a direct-fetched Land Bank disposition example: TWG says the project at 3620 Kechter Road provides 54 permanently affordable for-sale townhomes restricted to 80% AMI, and North Forty News reports the five-acre parcel was purchased for the city's land bank in 2002 and sold to the TWG / ECLT partnership for affordable home ownership.

For Proposition 123 land banking, Housing Catalyst reported a $1,610,000 award for Village on Eastbrook at 3221 Eastbrook Drive, tied to a 2.9-acre parcel and 73 affordable homes. The Governor's January 31, 2024 release lists Housing Catalyst's Village on Eastbrook as one of the first 16 Proposition 123 Land Banking recipients.

The city's Affordable Housing page says projects may qualify for fee credits when they provide housing for formerly homeless individuals, people with disabilities, or people earning 30% of AMI or less. The same page and Article 5 state the affordability thresholds for development incentives: rental projects must provide either 10% of units at 60% AMI or 20% at 80% AMI, while for-sale projects must provide either 10% at 80% AMI or 20% at 100% AMI.
The city also describes an impact-fee delay incentive: impact fees normally due at building permit may be delayed until certificate of occupancy or December 1 of that year, whichever comes first.

Colorado's HB24-1007 ended Fort Collins' U+2 framework in 2024. Collegian and CBS Colorado coverage report that Governor Jared Polis signed HB24-1007 on April 15, 2024, that the law took effect July 1, 2024, and that it barred local occupancy limits based on familial relationship. Collegian's July 2024 coverage says Fort Collins City Council then passed Ordinance 081 and Ordinance 082 on second reading by a 5-2 vote to remove occupancy limits from local codes.
